personality_voice.book_baseline: Bellatrix is defined by fanatical devotion to Voldemort expressed as near-erotic worship, explosive volatility, sadistic pleasure in cruelty, contempt for anyone she deems inferior (Mudbloods, blood traitors, half-bloods, even fellow Death Eaters she distrusts), and a theatrical, mocking quality in her speech. She oscillates between shrieking fury and cold menace. Her voice is characterized by rhetorical questions, sudden screams, mock-baby tones used to taunt, and passionate declarations of loyalty. She is also capable of sustained, pointed argument (the Spinner's End interrogation of Snape) and strategic thinking (recognizing the danger of the Sword of Gryffindor, stopping Lucius from summoning Voldemort prematurely). Her cruelty is not mindless — she enjoys it and is proud of it.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core traits: fanatical devotion to Voldemort ('I'd like to kill the boy'), explosive violence (stunning Snatchers instantly over the sword), theatrical mockery ('Come on, granny, give us your best'; 'Someone has to do the washing'), and the shrieking volatility. The mock-baby voice and taunting register are present. The Spinner's End scene preserves her distrust of Snape and her role as Bonder. The Malfoy Manor sequence preserves her sadism and her strategic alarm over the sword.
personality_voice.difference: The films compress or omit several speech patterns that build texture in the books: the extended Spinner's End interrogation is heavily cut, removing her sustained rhetorical challenge to Snape and her passionate self-justification ('He'd have me! I, who spent many years in Azkaban for him!'). Her mock-baby voice taunting Harry in the Ministry ('Come out, come out, little Harry!') is absent from the film. The film Bellatrix is slightly more physically theatrical and slightly less verbally complex. The Deathly Hallows Part 2 final duel line ('Be thankful we only took one... It's not like you don't have a spare') is a film invention that fits her cruelty but replaces the book's 'What will happen to your children when I've killed you? When Mummy's gone the same way as Freddie?' — a meaningful shift in register from taunting to cold cruelty.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The dominant traits — fanaticism, sadism, volatility, contempt, theatrical cruelty — are preserved across multiple scenes. The compression of Spinner's End removes some of her verbal complexity and self-righteous passion, but the core personality pattern is intact and consistently evidenced. Minor penalty for loss of the mock-baby taunting register and the compressed self-justification speech.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Bellatrix serves multiple narrative functions: primary antagonist to Harry and the Order in OotP (she kills Sirius, she is the target of Harry's pursuit and first Cruciatus attempt), instrument of Voldemort's will, torturer of the Longbottoms (establishing her as the series' emblem of Death Eater cruelty), key agent in the Malfoy Manor sequence (she controls the interrogation, identifies the sword's significance, tortures Hermione), and she dies at Molly Weasley's hands as a narrative payoff. She also functions as Bonder in the Unbreakable Vow scene. Her agency is consistently high: she initiates, decides, commands, and acts independently within Death Eater operations.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The films preserve her role as Sirius's killer, her function as Bonder, her command of the Malfoy Manor sequence, and her death at Molly's hands. She retains agency in the Malfoy Manor interrogation — she stuns Snatchers, commands the room, identifies the sword, and tortures Hermione. In the Battle of Hogwarts she is shown fighting independently and is the last Death Eater standing alongside Voldemort.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Ministry of Magic sequence in OotP is significantly compressed in the film. Bellatrix's extended pursuit of Harry through the Ministry, her taunting of him, her torture of Neville, and the sustained duel are all reduced. The film cuts her mock-baby taunting, her lesson to Harry about Unforgivable Curses ('You need to mean them, Potter!'), and her triumphant scream after killing Sirius is present but the chase is truncated. Her role in the Spinner's End scene is compressed — she loses the extended interrogation of Snape where she functions as a genuine challenger and ideological voice. In DH Part 1, the book's scene where she commands Greyback and manages the Malfoy Manor situation with strategic alarm is preserved, though some of her lines are redistributed or compressed.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Her core agency is preserved in the most important scenes. The compression of the Ministry sequence reduces her active role in that sequence but does not eliminate it. The Spinner's End compression is more significant — she loses her function as sustained ideological challenger to Snape. No major action is transferred to another character. Minor penalty for compressed agency in OotP Ministry and Spinner's End.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Bellatrix's primary motivation is absolute devotion to Voldemort — not merely obedience but something closer to worship and love. This is evidenced by her pride in her Azkaban imprisonment ('I, who spent many years in Azkaban for him!'), her near-erotic proximity to him at the Malfoy Manor table, her tears of delight at his attention, her terror when she thinks she has failed him ('MASTER, I TRIED, I TRIED — DO NOT PUNISH ME'), and her frenzied grief at his apparent death in the forest. Secondary motivations include blood-purity fanaticism (her rage at Harry's half-blood status, her disowning of Andromeda) and sadistic pleasure in cruelty as an end in itself. There is a meaningful internal tension: she is Voldemort's most devoted servant but is also capable of fear of his displeasure, which creates a vulnerability beneath the fanaticism.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The films preserve the devotion to Voldemort (volunteering to kill Harry, her proximity to him in the forest, 'My lord, let me help you'). Her blood-purity fanaticism is present ('You dare speak his name? You filthy half-blood!'). Her fear of Voldemort's displeasure is shown in DH Part 1 when she stops Lucius from summoning him prematurely. The sadistic pleasure is consistently shown.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The films lose the full depth of her devotion as a quasi-romantic obsession. The book's Malfoy Manor table scene — where her face floods with color and her eyes well with tears of delight at Voldemort's attention, and where she is publicly humiliated by the Tonks marriage joke — is absent from the film corpus provided. Her terror and self-abasement after the Ministry failure ('MASTER, I TRIED') is present in the book but not in the film corpus. The Spinner's End scene where she passionately defends her Azkaban imprisonment as proof of superior loyalty is heavily compressed in the film, losing the emotional texture of her motivation.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The core motivation — fanatical devotion — is present and recognizable in the films. The secondary motivations (blood purity, sadism) are intact. What is lost is the full emotional register of that devotion: the quasi-erotic worship, the public humiliation and shame, the terror of Voldemort's displeasure expressed in self-abasement. These are simplifications rather than replacements, but they flatten the motivational texture meaningfully.

character_arc.book_baseline: Bellatrix has a largely flat arc by design — she is introduced as a fanatical Death Eater and dies as one. Her arc is not one of change but of escalating consequence: she escapes Azkaban, kills Sirius, tortures Hermione, and is finally killed by Molly Weasley. The arc's dramatic function is the payoff of her accumulated cruelty. There is a minor arc of hubris: she is Voldemort's most trusted lieutenant but her failures (Ministry, Gringotts) erode that position, and she dies in the moment of apparent triumph. The arc is intentionally flat in terms of character development.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films preserve this flat arc faithfully: she escapes Azkaban, kills Sirius, participates in the Malfoy Manor torture, and is killed by Molly. The escalating consequence structure is intact. The hubris-and-fall element is present in compressed form.
character_arc.difference: The film omits the Malfoy Manor table scene (DH Part 1 book) where Voldemort publicly humiliates her over the Tonks marriage, which is a meaningful beat in her arc — a moment where her status as 'most loyal' is publicly mocked. This is a minor loss since the arc is intentionally flat, but it removes a moment of vulnerability and humiliation that complicates her triumphalism.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is flat by design and the films preserve that flatness faithfully. The omission of the humiliation scene is a minor loss. Full compression penalty is not warranted since the arc's key beats (escape, Sirius's death, Hermione's torture, Molly's killing blow) are all present.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three key relationships define Bellatrix: (1) Voldemort — quasi-devotional worship, the emotional center of her character; (2) Sirius — familial hatred and rivalry, she kills him with apparent triumph; (3) Narcissa — complex sisterly dynamic where Bellatrix is dominant, contemptuous of Narcissa's maternal weakness, yet ultimately serves as Bonder and is bound to her sister's fate. Secondary: her relationship with Neville (she destroyed his parents and he witnesses her cruelty directly) and with Harry (she is his primary antagonist in OotP, the target of his first Cruciatus attempt).
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Voldemort relationship is present but the quasi-erotic devotion is reduced to proximity and verbal deference. The Sirius relationship is preserved in its outcome (she kills him) but the familial dimension is not developed in the film corpus. The Narcissa relationship is preserved in the Spinner's End scene — the dynamic of Bellatrix as dominant, skeptical, and ultimately compliant is intact. The Neville relationship is present in OotP ('Neville Longbottom, is it? How's Mom and Dad?') but the extended torture scene is compressed. The Harry antagonist relationship is preserved.
key_relationships.difference: The Voldemort relationship loses its most emotionally intense moments — the tears of delight, the public humiliation, the self-abasement. The Sirius-Bellatrix familial dimension (they are cousins, she is on the Black family tree, Kreacher's devotion to her photograph) is entirely absent from the films. The Neville relationship loses the extended torture scene that makes her cruelty toward him personally meaningful rather than incidental.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The Narcissa and Harry relationships are adequately preserved. The Voldemort relationship is recognizable but emotionally flattened. The Sirius familial relationship is essentially absent from the films. The Neville relationship is compressed. These are meaningful losses that change the emotional texture of her key dynamics.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Bellatrix's complexity comes from several sources: (1) her sustained ideological challenge to Snape at Spinner's End, where she functions as a genuine intellectual adversary rather than just a screaming fanatic; (2) her strategic intelligence (recognizing the sword's significance, stopping Lucius from summoning Voldemort prematurely, managing the Malfoy Manor situation); (3) her vulnerability — the terror of Voldemort's displeasure, the public humiliation at the Malfoy Manor table, the moment of fear when Harry tells her the prophecy is smashed; (4) the quasi-erotic dimension of her devotion, which makes her fanaticism psychologically specific rather than generic; (5) her relationship with Kreacher and the Black family legacy; (6) the Neville dimension — she destroyed his parents and takes pleasure in reminding him of it.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve her strategic intelligence (Malfoy Manor sword scene), her sadism, her volatility, and her fanaticism. The Spinner's End scene preserves her distrust of Snape and her role as Bonder, though the extended interrogation is cut. The Neville confrontation in OotP is present but compressed.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: Lost: the full Spinner's End interrogation (her sustained ideological challenge, her passionate self-justification, her discomfiture when Snape outmaneuvers her); the Malfoy Manor table scene (public humiliation, tears of delight, the quasi-erotic devotion made visible); her terror and self-abasement after the Ministry failure; the Kreacher/Black family dimension; the extended Neville torture scene; her mock-baby taunting of Harry in the Ministry. These losses collectively flatten her from a psychologically specific fanatic with vulnerability and strategic intelligence into a more generically theatrical villain.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The losses are significant enough to warrant a meaningful penalty. The Spinner's End compression removes her most intellectually complex scene. The absence of the Malfoy Manor table scene removes the most emotionally specific expression of her devotion. The Kreacher/Black family material is entirely absent. However, the films do preserve enough of her strategic intelligence and sadistic pleasure to prevent a severe penalty. The character remains recognizable and substantially present, but the nuances that make her psychologically specific are noticeably reduced.

lost_or_transferred_material: Extended Spinner's End interrogation of Snape — Bellatrix's sustained ideological challenge, her passionate self-justification of Azkaban imprisonment as proof of superior loyalty, and her discomfiture when Snape outmaneuvers her
Malfoy Manor table scene (DH Part 1 book) — Bellatrix's tears of delight at Voldemort's attention, her public humiliation over the Tonks marriage, the quasi-erotic proximity to Voldemort
Ministry of Magic mock-baby taunting of Harry ('Come out, come out, little Harry!') and her lesson on Unforgivable Curses ('You need to mean them, Potter!')
Bellatrix's terror and self-abasement after the prophecy is smashed ('MASTER, I TRIED, I TRIED — DO NOT PUNISH ME')
Extended Neville torture scene in the Ministry — her sadistic pleasure in reminding Neville of his parents' fate and her deliberate prolonging of his suffering
Kreacher's devotion to Bellatrix's photograph and the Black family legacy dimension
Bellatrix's triumphant scream after killing Sirius is present in the film but the familial/Black family dimension of their relationship is entirely absent
Voldemort's public humiliation of Bellatrix over the Tonks marriage (a moment of vulnerability and shame)
score_caps_applied: Cap 2 (above 80 if key relationships change substantially) considered but not applied — relationships are altered but not substantially changed in their core function; the Narcissa and Harry relationships are preserved, and the Voldemort relationship is recognizable if emotionally flattened

Cap 1 (above 85 if major complexity is removed) applied implicitly — the score of 78 reflects the removal of the Spinner's End depth, the quasi-erotic devotion texture, and the Kreacher/Black family material, which together constitute a major portion of her psychological complexity
comparative.key_observations: The films deliver a recognizable and largely faithful Bellatrix Lestrange — her fanaticism, sadism, volatility, strategic intelligence, and theatrical cruelty are all present and consistently evidenced. The core personality pattern is intact. However, the adaptation loses the psychological specificity that makes her more than a theatrical villain: the quasi-erotic dimension of her Voldemort devotion, her sustained intellectual challenge to Snape at Spinner's End, her vulnerability and self-abasement when she fears Voldemort's displeasure, and the Black family legacy dimension are all significantly compressed or absent. The result is a Bellatrix who is faithful in broad strokes but flattened in the nuances that make her fanaticism psychologically specific and her cruelty personally motivated rather than generically theatrical. The score of 78 reflects a substantially faithful portrayal that nonetheless loses enough complexity and relational texture to prevent a higher rating.
